children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

8.I.1	The Office of Legal Affairs is responsible for providing a unified central legal service for the Organization; represents the Secretary-General on legal issues, including in legal conferences and in judicial proceedings; performs substantive and secretariat functions for organs involved in public international law, including the General Assembly and its Sixth Committee (Legal Committee), the International Law Commission and the United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL), as well as for the Assembly plenary meetings concerning oceans and the law of the sea; and performs the depositary functions of the Secretary-General for multilateral treaties and the functions of registration and publication of treaties. The structure and main functions of the Office are described in Secretary-General’s bulletin ST/SGB/2021/1.

8.I.2	The mandate derives from Articles 13, 98, 102, 104, 105 and other relevant provisions of the Charter of the United Nations, as well as the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions, including resolution 13 (I) of 13 February 1946, as the central legal service for the Organization (including funds and programmes and treaty bodies institutionally linked to the Organization). The Office discharges mandates from the priorities established in relevant Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolution 2205 (XXI), by which the Assembly established UNCITRAL to further the progressive harmonization and unification of the law of international trade, with the Office acting as its secretariat; resolution 49/28 and 52/26, by which the Assembly requested the Secretary-General to carry out responsibilities entrusted to him in the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea and related resolutions of the Assembly; and resolution 68/70, on oceans and the law of the sea, by which the Assembly established the Office as the focal point for UN Oceans. In addition, the Legal Counsel and the Office discharge the mandates derived from resolutions 77/321 and 78/272.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

8.I.3	The Office supports international law as the foundation for Member States to interact and cooperate in achieving their common goals. The Office will continue to respond to the needs of its stakeholders and beneficiaries with a specialized legal skill set, institutional memory, credibility and neutrality, while delivering on a diverse and complex mandate.

8.I.4	The Office will respond to the increasing demands for legal services from United Nations organs, including through the provision of advice on questions relating to the interpretation and application of international law instruments and on general questions of public international law, to ensure that legal considerations are an integral part of the Organization’s operations and the effective functioning of its principal and subsidiary organs. The Office will defend and protect the privileges and immunities of the United Nations in accordance with the Charter and the relevant conventions, including in national and international courts.

8.I.5	The Office will contribute to international justice and accountability, including through support for United Nations-assisted criminal tribunals and their oversight bodies, and other international accountability mechanisms.

8.I.6	The Office will continue to promote the wider appreciation and understanding of international law through its capacity-building activities, including those related to ocean affairs and the law of the sea, the United Nations Programme of Assistance in the Teaching, Study, Dissemination and Wider Appreciation of International Law and special initiatives such as subregional workshops on international law, workshops on treaty law and practice and those related to continuing to advance awareness and understanding of UNCITRAL texts, among others.

8.I.7	To further the progressive harmonization, modernization and unification of the law of international trade, the Office will ensure the widespread adoption and use of harmonized and modernized substantive private law rules to govern international commercial transactions, strengthen technical cooperation and promote the participation of developing countries in the law-making activities of UNCITRAL. The Office will support Member States in the preparation of universally acceptable legislative and non-legislative texts.

8.I.8	The Office will continue its role in supporting the Organization’s efforts to strengthen its response to allegations of sexual exploitation and abuse and to hold accountable those who have engaged in such behaviour. The Office will also support the legal aspects relating to the implementation of the Secretary-General’s strategic action plan on addressing racism and promoting dignity for all in the Secretariat.

8.I.9	The Office will support the initiatives of the Secretary-General envisioned to increase the Organization’s capacity, including by providing guidance on the legal aspects, to achieve a more agile, responsive and resilient United Nations, in particular by enhancing the Organization’s capabilities in innovation, data analytics, digital transformation, strategic foresight and behavioural science, in order to better support Member States and deliver on the Office’s mandates, in accordance with the Pact to the Future. In the context of the UN80 Initiative, the Office will continue to provide legal and procedural advice to address questions arising out of structural changes with a view to protecting the Organization from legal risks and liabilities and ensuring that reform is done in accordance with international law and the internal rules and regulations of the Organization.

8.I.10	With regard to the general legal services provided to United Nations organs and programmes, the Office will provide those services with a view to maximizing the protection of the legal interests of the Organization and minimizing its legal liabilities. The Office will advise on the legal aspects arising from United Nations institutional and operational activities and provide legal services for resolving disputes of a private law character involving the operations of the Organization, its subsidiary bodies and organs, and its separately administered funds and programmes.

8.I.11	The Office will also support the progressive development and codification of international law and the conclusion of legal instruments resulting in the promotion of universal respect for international law, including by continuing to serve as the secretariats of the Sixth Committee, the International Law Commission, the Special Committee on the Charter of the United Nations and on the Strengthening of the Role of the Organization, other special or ad hoc committees and diplomatic conferences convened to consider and formulate legal instruments. The Office will disseminate materials on international law through the United Nations Audiovisual Library of International Law and major legal publications.

8.I.12	The Office will engage in processes relating to oceans and the law of the sea to support the increased participation of States in and the effective implementation and application of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea and its implementing agreements. The Office will provide secretariat functions to oceans-related processes of the General Assembly and the Commission on the Limits of the Continental Shelf, as well as temporary secretariat functions related to the Agreement under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the Conservation and Sustainable Use of Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction. It will support the implementation and promotion of the Sustainable Development Goals, in particular Goal 14. The Office will also continue to deliver capacity-building activities on that topic, including for developing countries.

8.I.13	The Office will intensify the use of technological tools and other means to enhance the custody, registration and publication of treaties, including the registration of treaties and treaty actions in line with Article 102 of the Charter, and fulfil the depositary functions of the Secretary-General. The Office will continue to provide legal assistance and advice to States, specialized agencies, United Nations programmes and offices, treaty bodies and other entities on the law of treaties. The Office will also promote and ensure wider knowledge of the law of treaties, including through capacity-building activities and workshops, thereby preventing issues of interpretation and implementation of treaty provisions by contracting States.

8.I.14	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Office will continue to enable the International Law Commission to exchange knowledge, experience and ideas with the President of the International Court of Justice, the African Union Commission on International Law, the Inter-American Juridical Committee, the Asian-African Legal Consultative Organization and the Committee of Legal Advisers on Public International Law of the Council of Europe. The Office will also continue to cooperate with States and bodies established under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea. In the fields of the law of the sea and ocean governance and of international trade law, the Office will continue to cooperate with international financial institutions, donor agencies, intergovernmental organizations, non-governmental organizations and relevant communities, including Indigenous Peoples and local communities.

8.I.15	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Legal Counsel, as the focal point for UN-Oceans, will continue to coordinate the delivery of UN-Oceans outputs and promote cross-sectoral and integrated activities with the United Nations system, in particular with respect to capacity-building and in relation to the Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction. In addition, the Office will coordinate the work of organizations dealing with international trade law and promote the use of online resources, including those provided by other institutions, in the light of the closing of the dedicated trade law library. The Office will continue to foster and strengthen the networks of legal advisers of United Nations agencies, funds and programmes.
8.I.16	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	Extrabudgetary resources are available to complement the programme budget, allowing the Office to respond to the increased demand for its services;
(b)	Principal and subsidiary organs of the United Nations continue to request legal advice as a main component of the decision-making process;
(c)	Specific mandates relating to international trade law, oceans and law of the sea and others are renewed or confirmed by the competent intergovernmental bodies.

8.I.17	The Office integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. The Office will continue to promote gender equality and the empowerment of women throughout all its subprogrammes in the context of the Office’s capacity-building programmes and other related aspects.

8.I.18	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Office will integrate disability inclusion in its work, as appropriate. The Office will continue to take the steps necessary to ensure that, in the committees it services, delegates with disabilities are provided with the materials needed to facilitate their participation.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

8.I.19	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

8.I.20	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Legislative mandates

children.text: Evaluation activities

8.I.23	An evaluation on request management analytics to promote the typology of incoming requests to the Office and the better use of resources to improve the efficiency of the legal advice and input to beneficiaries is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Provision of legal services to the United Nations system as a whole
Objective
8.I.24	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure respect for and adherence to public international law, and the development of international justice and accountability.
Strategy
8.I.25	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide legal advice on the interpretation and application of the Charter, legal agreements and United Nations resolutions, rules and regulations, and public international law, including international human rights law, international humanitarian law and international criminal law;

(b)	Advise the United Nations and United Nations-assisted criminal tribunals and their oversight bodies, and other international accountability mechanisms, including on their founding arrangements, statutes, terms of reference and rules of procedure, and on the functions of the Secretary-General thereunder.
8.I.26	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A uniform and consistent practice of the law and, subsequently, in the effective functioning of the principal and subsidiary organs of the United Nations in accordance with international law;
(b)	The protection of the privileges and immunities of the United Nations;
(c)	The effective functioning of the United Nations and entities entrusted with mandates in the areas of international law;
(d)	The appointment and reappointment of the principals of the United Nations and United Nations-assisted international criminal tribunals and other international accountability mechanisms.
Programme performance in 2025
Legal frameworks for establishing a new United Nations field operation in Haiti and support for the multinational Gang Suppression Force in Haiti

8.I.27	The Security Council, in its resolution 2793 (2025), authorized Member States to establish a Gang Suppression Force mandated to neutralize, isolate, and deter gangs in Haiti and to support the Haitian National Police, the Haitian armed forces and national institutions. The Council also established the United Nations Support Office in Haiti to provide logistical and operational support, primarily to the Gang Suppression Force, as well as the United Nations Integrated Office in Haiti, the Haitian National Police and the Haitian armed forces, and, as required, technical support to the Organization of American States.

8.I.28	The subprogramme provided legal advice to inform the development of the planning directive for the new field mission. This included the preparation, review and clearance of operational documents of a legal nature and legal instruments relating to the grant, occupancy and use of land and premises, reimbursement frameworks for contingent-owned equipment in relation to the Gang Suppression Force, and the formulation of a draft agreement detailing the status, privileges, immunities and facilities applicable to the United Nations Support Office in Haiti and its personnel. The complexity of interaction between the United Nations field mission, the ad hoc multinational force and national security and military institutions requires comprehensive adjustments to the standard United Nations legal instruments for missions and adapting some practices of the Organization, including in relation to legal and financial liability. The subprogramme will continue to assist in these issues, as well as matters related to the implementation of the agreement, once concluded.

children.text: Result 3: advancement of organizational reforms and institutional transitions – Proposed programme plan for 2027

8.I.34	As the central legal service of the United Nations, the subprogramme provides legal advice on applicable rules and procedures, in particular on procedural and institutional law and privileges and immunities of the United Nations. The subprogramme supports the Organization through periods of transformation and reform initiatives, including within the framework of the UN80 Initiative. The subprogramme’s involvement is multifaceted, encompassing, among others, the provision of expert legal and procedural advice to address structural changes and institutional transitions. The subprogramme also offers critical guidance on complex legal challenges, in particular related to the safeguarding of the Organization’s interests and legal frameworks, including on privileges and immunities. The subprogramme will also support the implementation of any relevant decisions taken by legislative bodies, in connection with such changes and transitions.
Lessons learned and planned change

8.I.35	The lesson for the subprogramme was the importance of early and sustained engagement in reform processes. As organizational reforms and institutional transitions, including those under the UN80 Initiative, advanced, the subprogramme faced a continuous and significantly higher-than-anticipated demand for legal advice. The demand related to highly complex legal issues pertaining to, among others, procedural and institutional law and privileges and immunities of the United Nations. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme is refining the scope and nature of its engagement in organizational reforms and institutional transitions, thereby enhancing services by placing a greater emphasis on taking proactive steps to liaise closely with the relevant Secretariat departments and offices, to anticipate legal issues and to provide legal advice in a timely manner.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – General legal services provided to United Nations organs and programmes
Objective
8.I.38	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to maximize the protection of the legal interests of the Organization and to minimize its legal liabilities.
Strategy
8.I.39	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Assist in ensuring external accountability of United Nations and related personnel, including on internal sanctions and external enforcement action and liaison with national authorities;

(b)	Advise on the legal aspects arising from United Nations institutional and operational activities, including the Organization’s development, technical assistance, peacekeeping, humanitarian and other operations;

(c)	Assist the Organization, its organs or its separately administered funds and programmes by providing legal advice on procurement and substantial contracts and on operational and technical assistance activities of those entities;

(d)	Provide legal services for resolving disputes of a private law character involving the operations of the Organization, its organs or its funds and programmes, including representing the Organization in settlement negotiations and in arbitral proceedings;

(e)	Advise on management issues and matters in the system for the administration of justice, including determining whether to appeal against a judgment of the United Nations Dispute Tribunal, and represent the Secretary-General before the United Nations Appeals Tribunal;
(f)	Respond to requests by the Organization for urgent legal advice arising from humanitarian and emergency situations.
8.I.40	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The full maintenance of the status, legal rights and privileges and immunities of the Organization arising from United Nations operational activities;
(b)	The minimization of legal liabilities of the Organization.
Programme performance in 2025
Access by client offices to standard contract templates

8.I.41	To ensure that United Nations contracts maintain uniform provisions relating to the status and the privileges and immunities of the United Nations and its mode of operations as an intergovernmental organization, the subprogramme developed model contracts that accommodate the Organization’s need to operate globally. Such model contracts are now available on a single portal to officials of the United Nations system involved in the Organization’s procurement activities.

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Progressive development and codification of international law
Objective
8.I.51	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to progressively develop, codify and advance knowledge of international law.
Strategy
8.I.52	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide substantive support to the Sixth Committee, the International Law Commission, the Special Committee on the Charter of the United Nations and on the Strengthening of the Role of the Organization and other special or ad hoc committees and diplomatic conferences by assisting in proceedings, rendering legal advice, drafting legal instruments, resolutions and decisions and preparing background documents, analytical studies and reports;
(b)	Assist legal bodies of the General Assembly in the use by States of the procedures envisaged under the relevant resolutions of the Assembly;

(c)	Implement the mandates of the United Nations Programme of Assistance in the Teaching, Study, Dissemination and Wider Appreciation of International Law. This includes the planning, organization and conduct of four in-person training programmes, namely, the International Law Fellowship Programme and the United Nations Regional Courses in International Law for Africa, for Latin America and the Caribbean and for the Asia-Pacific region;

(d)	Further develop, disseminate and maintain the United Nations Audiovisual Library of International Law and prepare and disseminate major legal publications and information on the legal work of the United Nations;
(e)	Support Member States by assisting the bureaux of the bodies that the subprogramme services in devising and implementing working methods and work programmes that ensure business continuity.
8.I.53	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The smooth deliberations of legislative and legal bodies, the conclusion of draft legal instruments and the promotion of universal respect for international law;
(b)	The wider awareness and appreciation of international law by a wider audience.
Programme performance in 2025
Special Committee on the Charter of the United Nations and on the Strengthening of the Role of the Organization adopted a substantive report

8.I.54	From 2022 to 2024 the Special Committee on the Charter of the United Nations and on the Strengthening of the Role of the Organization was unable to reach consensus on a substantive report and was able to reach consensus only on a technical report. In 2025, the subprogramme provided substantive guidance and advice to the Bureau in the preparation of the draft substantive report in such a manner that allowed for the adoption of a substantive report by consensus, thereby fully satisfying the mandate of the Special Committee and restoring the Special Committee’s prior practice.

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Law of the sea and ocean affairs
Objective

8.I.64	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen the law of the sea for the peaceful use of the oceans and for their conservation and sustainable use, and as the basis for national, regional and global action and cooperation in the marine sector.
Strategy
8.I.65	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide expert advice to States and international organizations on the law of the sea and ocean affairs, in particular with respect to the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea of 1982, the Agreement for the Implementation of the Provisions of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea of 10 December 1982 relating to the Conservation and Management of Straddling Fish Stocks and Highly Migratory Fish Stocks of 1995, the Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction, and the work of the General Assembly on oceans and the law of the sea and sustainable fisheries;
(b)	Support the implementation of the Convention and the United Nations Fish Stocks Agreement in respect of sustainable fisheries-related cooperative activities among States and other entities;

(c)	Provide secretariat services to intergovernmental bodies, including the Commission on the Limits of the Continental Shelf and as interim secretariat under the Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction;

(d)	Undertake capacity-building activities (fellowships, training courses and workshops) relating to oceans and the law of the sea in collaboration with relevant stakeholders, including to promote a better understanding of the Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction.
8.I.66	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	An increased number of States parties to the Convention and its implementing agreements and their effective implementation;
(b)	Enhanced cooperation and coordination among stakeholders on ocean and coastal issues, leading to enhanced conservation and sustainable use of the oceans and their resources;

(c)	Enhanced human resource capacity of Member States, in particular developing countries, to implement the legal regime for the ocean under the Convention and other relevant international instruments effectively.
Programme performance in 2025
Required conditions met for the entry into force of the Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction

8.I.67	The Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction was adopted by consensus in June 2023 and required the deposit of 60 instruments of ratification, approval, acceptance or accession to enter into force. The subprogramme, in accordance with General Assembly resolution 77/321, undertook an extensive programme of activities to promote a better understanding of the Agreement in support of the efforts of States to become Parties to the Agreement, including the convening of regional workshops, the organization of briefings and events, the provision of technical assistance and the publication of materials. The conditions for entry into force were met on 19 September 2025 with the deposit of the sixtieth instrument of ratification by a State, a little over two years after the Agreement was adopted.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened role of the Commission on the Limits of the Continental Shelf and its subcommissions in accordance with the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea – Proposed programme plan for 2027

8.I.73	In its resolutions 78/69 and 80/110, the General Assembly recognized: (a) the importance of the delineation by coastal States of the outer limits of the continental shelf beyond 200 nautical miles in accordance with the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea and of the role of the Commission on the Limits of the Continental Shelf in that regard; (b) the importance of the work of the Commission for coastal States and for the international community; and (c) noted with concern the projected timetable of the work of the Commission on the submissions already received by it and those yet to be received. To that effect, the Assembly noted the need for enhanced support and assistance to the Commission.
Lessons learned and planned change

8.I.74	The lesson for the subprogramme was that regular assessment of the specialized services provided to the Commission, including through consultation with the Commission and the States Parties to the Convention, could help to ensure that the subprogramme addressed the Commission’s evolving needs. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme, in consultation with the Commission and States Parties to the Convention, and based on the assessment of the needs, will improve the effectiveness of services and assistance by providing enhanced geospatial information management support.

Subprogramme 5 – Progressive harmonization, modernization and unification of the law of international trade
Objective
8.I.77	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to further the progressive harmonization and unification of the law of international trade.
Strategy
8.I.78	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Serve as the secretariat for UNCITRAL, provide Member States with working papers, draft reports and draft substantive private law rules on international trade, and give advice on and assistance in intergovernmental negotiations and coordinate those activities with equivalent activities of other international organizations in accordance with the UNCITRAL mandate and propose that UNCITRAL recommend the use or adoption of relevant instruments from other organizations;

(b)	Provide technical assistance and capacity-building to Governments, including diagnostic assessments, implementation guidance and interpretative materials, as well as training activities and advice on the enactment of UNCITRAL texts in national law;

(c)	Conduct research and facilitate the exchange of experience among Member States on reinforcing the resilience of international trade and global supply chains, with a particular focus on micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises, and explore possible future legislative development to meet the challenges identified for consideration by the Member States.
8.I.79	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Well-informed, transparent and inclusive deliberations of delegations in working groups and the Commission;
(b)	Governments agreeing on substantive private law rules governing international trade and their reflection in universally acceptable instruments;
(c)	The availability of additional UNCITRAL instruments to harmonize and unify international trade law and to encourage resilience in international trade;
(d)	Increased government use and adoption of UNCITRAL instruments in national law and increased legal decisions on those instruments;
(e)	Increased treaty actions, enactments and legal decisions based on UNCITRAL texts.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened judicial capacity in applying guidance provided in United Nations Commission on International Trade Law insolvency law texts

8.I.80	Improved knowledge of the UNCITRAL insolvency law texts can support the more effective handling of domestic insolvency cases. On the basis of a request by UNCITRAL, the subprogramme, in partnership with the World Bank Group, has implemented the biennial online Judicial Capacity-Building Initiative on International Best Practices in the Area of Insolvency Law since 2021. Building on the previous editions, the third edition, in 2025, continued to provide a global platform for judicial engagement on using UNCITRAL insolvency law texts. For the first time, interpretation was available in all six United Nations official languages, broadening accessibility across diverse linguistic and legal environments, which attracted significant worldwide interest, with participants attending from 55 jurisdictions, an increase from 30 in 2023.

8.I.81	Sustained participation across editions reflected continued demand for practical guidance on the interpretation of UNCITRAL insolvency law frameworks. In 2025, participants strengthened their understanding of international best practices, contributing to the more effective handling of domestic cases and reinforcing dialogue and cooperation among various jurisdictions.

children.text: Result 3: implementation of the reforms to investor-State dispute settlement through a multilateral framework convention mechanism – Proposed programme plan for 2027

8.I.87	The subprogramme has worked on the possible reform of investor-State dispute settlement since 2017 through an inclusive, State-driven process. While several reform elements have been finalized, the means for their implementation remain under development. Consideration of a multilateral framework convention emerged as a procedural option to enable States to consent to and apply the reform elements in a flexible manner.
Lessons learned and planned change

8.I.88	The lesson for the subprogramme was that a shared understanding of implementation mechanisms could help to achieve consensus among member States on the reform elements. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will place a greater emphasis on informal sessions and intersessional consultations to facilitate understanding and consensus. Through these efforts, the Working Group III is expected to present an adaptable and comprehensive approach to implementing investor-State dispute settlement reforms.

Subprogramme 6 – Custody, registration and publication of treaties
Objective

8.I.91	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the transparency of the international treaty framework, wider knowledge of the law of treaties and broader participation in multilateral treaties concluded under the auspices of the United Nations and deposited with the Secretary-General.
Strategy
8.I.92	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Undertake the registration and publication of treaties and treaty actions submitted under Article 102 of the Charter of the United Nations and provide timely and accurate information relating to deposited treaties, registered treaties and related actions through the electronic dissemination of treaty-related information on the online United Nations Treaty Collection and the publication of the United Nations Treaty Series;

(b)	Provide legal assistance and advice to States, specialized agencies, United Nations programmes and offices, treaty bodies and other entities on the law of treaties, including final clauses of treaties, the depositary practice of the Secretary-General and the registration and publication of treaties. Such assistance would be provided in particular during the negotiation of multilateral treaties and through the holding of capacity-building seminars at Headquarters and at the national and regional levels, as well as through the elaboration of topical reference publications;

(c)	Fulfil the depositary functions of the Secretary-General in respect of more than 600 multilateral treaties and hold and conduct treaty events, including during the high-level week of the annual session of the General Assembly.
8.I.93	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Improved access to treaties deposited with the Secretary-General and related treaty actions, including information on their status, and to treaties and related treaty actions submitted for registration and publication with the Secretariat;

(b)	The prevention of issues of interpretation and implementation of final clauses of multilateral treaties by contracting States and enhanced familiarity with and understanding by States of the technical and legal aspects of participating in the multilateral treaty framework and registering treaties;
(c)	The entry into force of multilateral treaties deposited with the Secretary-General and the universal participation of States in such treaties.
Programme performance in 2025

Opening for signature of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime; Strengthening International Cooperation for Combating Certain Crimes Committed by Means of Information and Communications Technology Systems and for the Sharing of Evidence in Electronic Form of Serious Crimes

8.I.94	In its resolution 79/243, the General Assembly adopted the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime; Strengthening International Cooperation for Combating Certain Crimes Committed by Means of Information and Communications Technology Systems and for the Sharing of Evidence in Electronic Form of Serious Crimes. Its opening for signature ceremony was organized by the subprogramme and hosted by the Government of Viet Nam in Hanoi on 25 and 26 October 2025.

8.I.95	Following the adoption of the Convention, the subprogramme prepared the original text of the Convention and issued and circulated certified true copies for the completion of national processes by States and regional economic integration organizations. It also provided procedural information on the opening for signature of the Convention and reviewed instruments of full powers. The subprogramme supported the participation of representatives of States and organizations in the ceremony. The Secretary-General’s depositary notifications and official pictures of ceremonies were circulated in a timely manner.

text: II.	Independent Investigative Mechanism for Myanmar

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

8.II.1	The Independent Investigative Mechanism for Myanmar is responsible for collecting, consolidating, preserving and analysing evidence of the most serious international crimes and violations of international law committed in Myanmar since 2011. The Mechanism is further responsible for preparing files that can be shared with competent investigative, prosecutorial and judicial authorities (“competent authorities”) in order to facilitate fair and independent criminal proceedings in national, regional or international courts or tribunals. The mandate derives from relevant resolutions of the Human Rights Council, including resolution 39/2, and of the General Assembly, including resolution 73/264, and is further elaborated in the Mechanism’s terms of reference (A/73/716, annex).
Programme of work
Objective

8.II.2	The objective, to which the Mechanism contributes, is to ensure the accountability of perpetrators and justice for victims of the most serious international crimes and violations of international law committed in Myanmar since 2011.
Strategy and external factors for 2027
8.II.3	To contribute to the objective, the Mechanism will:

(a)	Collect, preserve and analyse information and evidence regarding crimes within its mandate for current and future accountability efforts, prioritizing the gravest incidents, including by undertaking investigative missions;
(b)	Safeguard the well-being and safety of witnesses and others who cooperate with the Mechanism, including through cooperation with national authorities and other partners;

(c)	Adapt its secure electronic information management system to protect against evolving risks to the security of digital systems and data and to facilitate the analysis of vast quantities of data in multiple formats and languages;

(d)	Share evidence and analysis with competent authorities in accordance with its mandate, with the informed consent of those who provided the information, to facilitate criminal proceedings and other accountability efforts;

(e)	Engage in dialogue and coordination with stakeholders, including Member States, United Nations actors, civil society and affected communities, to facilitate greater cooperation with the Mechanism and promote accountability efforts.
8.II.4	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Competent authorities having access, upon request and in a timely manner, to high-quality and reliable evidence and analysis stored in the Mechanism’s secure electronic information management system with enhanced processes for review, collation and distribution;
(b)	An increased number of fair and independent criminal proceedings and other accountability efforts and expanded use by competent authorities of the Mechanism’s evidence and analysis;
(c) 	Witnesses and others cooperating with the Mechanism securely and with the necessary psychosocial support;
(d)	Deeper understanding by relevant stakeholders, in particular affected communities and civil society, of the work of the Mechanism, facilitating strengthened cooperation with the Mechanism.
8.II.5	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Some relevant Member States will permit the Mechanism to conduct evidence collection and witness interviews on their territory and will facilitate the safety and well-being of witnesses and information providers;
(b)	Some national or international judicial authorities will exercise jurisdiction over the crimes within the Mechanism’s mandate and such authorities will request information from the Mechanism;
(c)	Extrabudgetary resources will be available to address evolving challenges and sustain specialized capacities, including open-source investigations.

8.II.6	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Mechanism will continue to request access to States for evidence-gathering and to cooperate with competent authorities, civil society organizations and business entities to seek relevant information and ensure support for its mandate.

8.II.7	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Mechanism will continue to consult and cooperate with other United Nations entities working on issues related to Myanmar and other United Nations accountability mechanisms, in particular in the areas of legal and judicial cooperation, information management systems, investigation of sexual and gender-based crimes, witness protection and support, and the development of methodologies and policies.

8.II.8	The Mechanism integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. The Mechanism will continue to investigate sexual and gender-based crimes and crimes against children within its mandate, share relevant evidence and analysis with competent authorities and facilitate access to protection and support measures for victims of such crimes.
8.II.9	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Mechanism will actively ensure appropriate access to protection and support measures to witnesses with disabilities.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

8.II.10	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the table of deliverables reflects updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

8.II.11	The Mechanism will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including the decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Evaluation activities
8.II.12	An evaluation conducted by the Mechanism, on the investigative mission planning process and implementation, was completed in 2025 and has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

8.II.13	In response to the results of the evaluation, the Mechanism plans to adjust its mission planning framework to reflect the Mechanism’s complex operations, including high-security and witness protection requirements, the need for the cooperation of Member States and other stakeholders, and logistical considerations. The evaluation will also inform efforts to consolidate and streamline existing operating procedures and improve efficiencies in mission-related budgeting and expense arrangements.
8.II.14	The following evaluations to be conducted by the Mechanism are planned for 2027 (postponed from 2026):
(a) 	Evaluation of the Mechanism’s methodology for sharing information with national authorities and related outcomes;
(b)	Evaluation of the Mechanism’s witness protection and support measures.
Programme performance in 2025
Competent authorities have access to forensic analysis of open-source information

8.II.15	There is a significant volume of open-source information online and, more broadly, in the public domain regarding serious international crimes committed in Myanmar. Owing to the critical importance of promptly identifying and capturing such information, the Mechanism prioritized and integrated the collection and analysis of open-source information across all investigations. The Mechanism uses advanced methods that include the discovery, verification and authentication of large volumes of open-source materials, including videos, photographs, geospatial imagery, audio clips, public documents, official pronouncements and various databases and platforms. The Mechanism’s methodologies optimize the forensic value of open-source information to criminal prosecutions. For example, by using specialized tools, the Mechanism can identify where and when videos and images were taken, extract investigative leads from large data sets and enhance and authenticate videos, images and audio clips to detect “fake” content generated by artificial intelligence. In addition, the Mechanism can identify public statements by individuals that reveal their knowledge, intent or effective control over crimes to help to establish links between senior leaders and the crimes committed. The Mechanism analyses and cross-references open-source evidence with other evidence and prepares analysis that is available for sharing with competent authorities. Given the Mechanism’s lack of access to the crime scenes, open-source investigations are crucial to help to build cases for future prosecutions. Taking into account reduced extrabudgetary resources, the Mechanism prioritized the production of in-depth analysis, including incident reports, geolocation, chronolocation, data visualization and target profiles.
8.II.16	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 8.II.I).
Figure 8.II.I – Performance measure: number of information items collected and analysis produced from open sources for use by competent authorities (cumulative)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: victims, survivors, civil society and information providers engage in accountability processes – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

8.II.17	The programme’s work contributed to 23 engagements of victims, survivors, civil society representatives and information providers in 2025, for a cumulative total of 153 engagements between 2023 and 2025, which exceeded the planned target of 147 engagements.
8.II.18	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 8.II.II).
Figure 8.II.II – Performance measure: number of engagements of victims, survivors, civil society representatives and information providers (cumulative)
Result 2: increased access to evidence with the broadest possible admissibility in national, regional and international courts – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

8.II.19	The programme’s work contributed to 125 screening notes and witness statements available for sharing, for a cumulative total of 666 screening and statements, which exceeded the planned target of 636 screenings and statements.
8.II.20	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 8.II.III).
Figure 8.II.III – Performance measure: number of screening notes and witness statements available for sharing (cumulative)
Result 3: witnesses and sources contribute to accountability efforts securely and with necessary psychosocial support – Proposed programme plan for 2027

8.II.21	Investigating serious international crimes presents considerable security, safety and psychosocial risks for witnesses, victims and other sources who cooperate with the Mechanism. In line with its terms of reference, the Mechanism has a small internal capacity and is operationalizing international best practices in its witness protection services. The Mechanism takes all feasible steps to safeguard the safety and well-being of witnesses before, during and after their cooperation with the Mechanism. These measures are embedded across all aspects of the Mechanism’s investigations and sharing activities.
Lessons learned and planned change

8.II.22	The lesson for the Mechanism was that there was a need to respond to an increased number of witnesses to be interviewed and to address the complexities surrounding witness protection in various locations where the witnesses resided. The number of witnesses and sources requiring ongoing protection has grown steadily, leading to an annual rise in risk assessments and other measures. In applying the lesson, the Mechanism will adjust its policy framework and approach for witness protection. This will ensure that the security and psychosocial needs of witnesses are assessed jointly and comprehensively, with concrete recommendations for mitigating measures in line with international standards. Leveraging its electronic information management system, the Mechanism will revise internal procedures and streamline the risk assessment process to ensure that all witness protection activities are conducted efficiently.

text: III.	International, Impartial and Independent Mechanism to Assist in the Investigation and Prosecution of Persons Responsible for the Most Serious Crimes under International Law Committed in the Syrian Arab Republic since March 2011

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

8.III.1	The Mechanism is responsible for assisting in the investigation and prosecution of persons responsible for the most serious international crimes committed in the Syrian Arab Republic since March 2011. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolution 71/248, as further elucidated in the Mechanism’s terms of reference (A/71/755, annex). The Mechanism has a responsibility to support criminal accountability and in doing so provides support to ongoing investigations and prosecutions in competent jurisdictions. It also generates analytical products that can be shared with competent jurisdictions that either currently, or may in the future, have jurisdiction over the crimes within the Mechanism’s mandate.
Programme of work
Objective
8.III.2	The objective, to which the Mechanism contributes, is to achieve accountability for the most serious international crimes committed in the Syrian Arab Republic since March 2011.
Strategy and external factors for 2027
8.III.3	To contribute to the objective, the Mechanism will:

(a)	Build the central repository through existing or new cooperation agreements and focused investigative work and analysis of evidence within the framework of its structural investigation and in response to requests for assistance from competent jurisdictions;
(b)	Prepare analytical products in the context of projects within active lines of inquiry in its structural investigation;
(c)	Share information, evidence, analytical products and expertise with national, regional or international courts and tribunals that have jurisdiction over these alleged crimes;

(d)	Develop and implement its victim- and survivor-centred approach and integrate a focus on gender-based violence, including sexual violence, and crimes against children throughout its work, as well as addressing broader justice objectives, such as assisting efforts to clarify the fate of missing persons;

(e)	Promote an integrated and inclusive approach to justice and accountability through effective dialogue and coordination, including with criminal justice actors, civil society and other United Nations actors;
(f)	Provide support and protection to witnesses who cooperate with the Mechanism to ensure that they can do so in safety and security;

(g)	Engage with the Government of the Syrian Arab Republic, within the scope of its mandate, and other accountability actors in supporting justice processes in the Syrian Arab Republic for victims and survivors.
8.III.4	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Requesting parties having access to crucial evidence of alleged crimes, stored in a central repository and available for collation and distribution;
(b)	Strengthened investigations, prosecutions and trials by jurisdictions with competence over the alleged crimes within the Mechanism’s mandate;

(c)	Greater accessibility to, and influence over, justice processes for victims and survivors, including those disadvantaged by gender-based and/or age-related discrimination, of crimes in the Syrian Arab Republic;
(d)	The strengthening of the broadest possible range of justice initiatives compatible with the Mechanism’s mandate;
(e) 	An effective transitional justice process in the Syrian Arab Republic.
8.III.5	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	Entities and individuals will provide and/or facilitate the Mechanism’s access to information and evidence in various formats for inclusion in the central repository;
(b)	Access to the territory of the Syrian Arab Republic will be granted to the Mechanism for operations in furtherance of its mandate.

8.III.6	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Mechanism will continue to request access to a growing number of States for evidence-gathering as well as to cooperate with competent jurisdictions, international organizations, Syrian and international civil society organizations and individuals, utilizing a variety of cooperation frameworks.

8.III.7	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Mechanism will continue to cooperate with other United Nations entities working on issues related to the Syrian Arab Republic, including the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons, the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Syrian Arab Republic and the Independent Institution on Missing Persons in the Syrian Arab Republic, in accordance with its mandate.

8.III.8	The Mechanism integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. For example, the Mechanism continues to incorporate a gender perspective into its evidentiary strategies to avoid bias that results in the incomplete or inaccurate recording of harm experienced by females and to ensure that due attention is paid to sexual violence against males.

8.III.9	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Mechanism will integrate throughout its work the experiences and perspectives of persons with disabilities in the Syrian context. This is consistent with its victim- and survivor-centred approach, whereby the Mechanism seeks to bring to light and address the experiences of a broad range of victims and survivors who have been historically overlooked.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

8.III.10	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

8.III.11	The Mechanism will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Evaluation activities
8.III.12	An evaluation conducted by the entity on the design and implementation of the Mechanism’s strategic plan during the period 2023–2025 has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

8.III.13	In response to the lessons learned of the evaluation referenced above, the Mechanism finalized its strategic plan for the period 2026–2028, which better incorporates avenues to enable the Mechanism to increase its engagement with the Government of the Syrian Arab Republic as it rebuilds its justice processes.
8.III.14	An evaluation of the Mechanism’s implementation of the data protection and privacy policy for the Secretariat of the United Nations (ST/SGB/2024/3) is planned for 2027.
Programme performance in 2025
Steps towards Syrian-led transitional justice

8.III.15	The end of the Assad regime in December 2024 presented an opportunity for the Mechanism to engage with the Government of the Syrian Arab Republic on accountability. The Mechanism supported Syrian-led transitional justice efforts by sharing knowledge and expertise related to aspects of the Mechanism’s mandate, including evidence preservation and analysis and insights from its engagement with competent jurisdictions, civil society, and victims and survivors. The Mechanism conducted regular diplomatic missions to Damascus, which included meetings with the Ministry of Justice, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and other justice actors. In July 2025, the Mechanism hosted members of the national committee on transitional justice in Geneva to discuss how the Mechanism could support the implementation of its mandate and participated in a forum on transitional justice with the same members and civil society convened by the International Center for Transitional Justice. The Mechanism also participated in various engagements in the Syrian Arab Republic, notably the day of dialogue hosted by the European Union in Damascus in close coordination with the Government of the Syrian Arab Republic and civil society, and an event on justice and accountability for chemical weapons use in the Syrian Arab Republic with Syrian justice actors.

Result 3: Syrian-led, Syrian-owned truth, justice and accountability for the most serious crimes under international law committed in the Syrian Arab Republic since 2011 – Proposed programme plan for 2027

8.III.21	To advance accountability for the most serious international crimes committed in the Syrian Arab Republic since March 2011, the Mechanism established initial relationships with Syrian justice actors starting in December 2024, at the end of the Assad regime.
Lessons learned and planned change

8.III.22	The lesson for the Mechanism was the need to adapt to the evolving context of Syrian-led accountability efforts and judicial infrastructure in the Syrian Arab Republic and identify forms of support uniquely tailored to the needs and priorities of Syrian justice actors and victims and survivors. In applying the lesson, the Mechanism will support justice processes in third-country national and international jurisdictions while ramping up support for the Government of the Syrian Arab Republic in accordance with the Government’s progress and priorities. The Mechanism will support the Government through knowledge transfer exercises, including meetings and workshops regarding its working methods, and through the provision of materials and services that it provides to judicial actors subject to the terms of its mandate.
